## Core Rules
- **Default**: Use beads for ALL task tracking (`bd create`, `bd ready`, `bd close`)
- **Prohibited**: Do NOT use TodoWrite, TaskCreate, or markdown files for task tracking
- **Workflow**: Create beads issue BEFORE writing code, mark in_progress when starting

## Essential Commands

### Finding Work
- `bd ready` - Show issues ready to work (no blockers)
- `bd list --status=open` - All open issues
- `bd list --status=in_progress` - Your active work
- `bd show <id>` - Detailed issue view with dependencies

### Creating & Updating
- `bd create --title="Summary of this issue" --description="Why this issue exists and what needs to be done" --type=task|bug|feature --priority=2` - New issue

- `bd update <id> --assignee=username` - Assign to someone

- `bd close <id>` - Mark complete

# The sparq vectorisation estate ‚Äî technical map & reusability assessment for the Kernel of Truth

**Scope.** Read-only survey of `/home/ec2-user/css/kernel/sparq` (crate `sparq-vectors`, plus
`sparq-canon`, `sparq-zk`, and the vector design records under `research/`), assessed against the
kernel programme's need: a **deterministic, training-free NSM-explication ‚Üí vector encoder** with a
`(concept-hash ‚Üí embedding)` table and a decode path back to consumable objects
(design doc: `/home/ec2-user/css/kernel/concept-gist/concept-hash-design.md`, esp. ¬ß3‚Äì¬ß6, ¬ß9a).

**Method note (empirical honesty).** Every claim below is grounded in code as it exists on disk,
cited `file:line`. Where the sparq design docs *plan* something the code does not do, that is said
explicitly. One tasking correction up front: `crates/sparq-serve/src/canon.rs` is **not** RDFC
canonicalisation ‚Äî it is SPARQL *query-text* cache-key canonicalisation (whitespace collapse +
optional variable alpha-renaming; `crates/sparq-serve/src/canon.rs:1-43`). RDFC-1.0 lives in the
standalone crate **`sparq-canon`** (`crates/sparq-canon/src/lib.rs:1-30`) and is consumed by
`sparq-zk` for commitments.

---

## 1. Deterministic vs trained: the exact split

`sparq-vectors` is architected so that **embeddings are normally produced out-of-process** and the
crate itself is a search/store/import surface (`crates/sparq-vectors/src/embed.rs:1-5`,
`src/structure.rs:10-17`). Almost everything *in-tree* is deterministic; the trained parts are
either external (remote embedding API) or an explicitly research-grade opt-in trainer.

### 1.1 Deterministic, training-free components (same input ‚Üí same vector, no weights)

**(a) `HashEmbedder` ‚Äî deterministic hashed text projection** (`src/embed.rs:26-102`).
Test-only by declaration, but fully deterministic and cross-platform: per token (word +
character-trigram over whitespace-normalised text), a 64-bit **FNV-1a** hash XOR a seed feeds a
**SplitMix64** stream that contributes ¬±1 to each of `dim` components; the accumulated vector is
L2-normalised (`embed.rs:41-72`, splitmix64 at `embed.rs:96-102`, default seed
`0x5354_5156_5645_4354` at `embed.rs:33`). Documented honestly: "It captures NO semantics ('car'
and 'automobile' are unrelated); do not ship it as a retrieval feature" (`embed.rs:21-25`).
Determinism is pinned by test (`embed.rs:679-687`). This is a signed random-projection over hashed
tokens ‚Äî the exact trick reusable for hashing *concept-hash* terminals into fixed-width lanes.

**(b) The typed-literal encoders (structure-aware P1)** ‚Äî pure functions keyed by datatype, "No
training, no graph state, no I/O" (`src/encode.rs:19-21`):

- **Datatype router** `route(datatype) -> Encoder` ‚Äî exact dispatch on the datatype IRI alone:
  numeric family (incl. all derived integer subtypes) ‚Üí `Numeric`, `xsd:boolean` ‚Üí `Boolean`,
  date/dateTime/dateTimeStamp/gYear ‚Üí `Date`, everything else (strings, langStrings, IRIs) ‚Üí
  `Other`/text lane (`encode.rs:149-166`; enum and taxonomy blocks carry their own tags,
  `encode.rs:114-129`).
- **Order-preserving numeric encoder** (`NumericEncoder`, `encode.rs:250-333`): (1) fit an
  empirical per-predicate range from observed values (`fit`, `encode.rs:267-271`; `normalise`
  currently linear over `[min,max]` with clamping, `encode.rs:286-305`); (2) map the normalised
  scalar through a **strictly-monotone thermometer/cumulative code**: `dim[i] = clamp((u ‚àí i/d)¬∑d,
  0, 1)` (`encode.rs:343-350`). The provable invariant: L2 distance between codes is globally
  monotone in value distance ‚Äî "30 is nearer 31 than to 70, across the full range" ‚Äî checked by the
  exported metamorphic property test `metamorphic_monotone` (`encode.rs:768-801`), which a periodic
  Fourier code would fail by design (`encode.rs:44-48`). Order-preservation is exact under **L2,
  not cosine** (`encode.rs:50-55`).
- **Boolean sign encoder**: one reserved ¬±1 dimension; `decode(slot) = slot >= 0.0` is an exact
  round-trip (`encode.rs:359-387`).
- **Date encoder**: temporal lexical ‚Üí epoch-seconds scalar (`temporal_value`,
  `encode.rs:185-202`, XPath semantics via sparq-core `Timeline`, gYear handled directly) ‚Üí the
  same thermometer lane (`encode.rs:400-429`).
- **Enum codebook** (`src/codebook.rs:39-133`): one-hot over a *closed* `sh:in`/`owl:oneOf` member
  set, width = members + 1, slot 0 reserved for the out-of-enum **invalid code**; membership is a
  **slot match, never a cosine threshold**; `encode`/`decode` round-trip every member
  (`codebook.rs:9-23, 123-133`).
- **QUDT unit normalisation** (`src/units.rs:103-208`): a curated affine table
  (`canonical = lexical¬∑factor + offset`) to canonical SI per `QuantityKind`; unknown/compound
  units **fail closed** (`None`, never treated as dimensionless, `units.rs:180-200`); quantity
  kind is a routing slot so a length never shares a block with a mass (`units.rs:44-76, 173-178`).

**(c) The self-describing row layout ‚Äî `SchemaHeader` / `.spqs`** (`encode.rs:532-709`). A node
vector is a **structured partitioned object**: contiguous, non-overlapping `Block`s each carrying
`(encoder tag, metric tag, offset, width, optional fusion weight)` (`encode.rs:469-513`).
Serialises to a deterministic little-endian byte string with magic `SPQS`, version 2
(`encode.rs:551-564, 629-644`); parsing is fail-closed (bad magic/version/tag/partition ‚Üí `Err`,
`encode.rs:646-709`). The **metric-correctness guard** `check_euclidean` makes "cosine on a
non-Euclidean block" a detectable error, not silent corruption (`encode.rs:604-622`).

**(d) The taxonomy block (structure-aware P3)** (`src/taxonomy.rs`). `TaxonomyDag::build` extracts
the `rdfs:subClassOf` DAG from a (closed) graph with a **deterministic dense class order**
(sorted dict ids, `taxonomy.rs:95-133`), cycle-safe ancestor/depth computation
(`taxonomy.rs:157-209`). Two closed-form encoders:

- `EuclideanTaxonomyEncoder` (the default): `out[0]` = normalised depth; `out[1..]` = a **hashed
  ancestor bag** ‚Äî each proper ancestor's stable class id hashes into one of `bag_dim` coordinates,
  then the bag lane is L2-normalised so distance reflects ancestry *overlap* not raw count
  (`taxonomy.rs:246-331`; width = `bag_dim + 1`, default `bag_dim = 16`).
- `HyperbolicTaxonomyEncoder`: a **closed-form, untrained** Poincar√©-disc placement (radius from
  depth, angle hashed from class id ‚Äî `taxonomy.rs:337-390`), tagged `Metric::NonEuclidean`. It
  exists so the **`GeometryGate`** has a real second arm: the gate *measures* average distortion
  `|d_embed/d_graph ‚àí 1|` of both geometries on the actual DAG and picks Euclidean unless
  hyperbolic strictly wins by a margin (default 5%) (`taxonomy.rs:405-520`). Adoption of
  non-Euclidean geometry is measurement-gated by construction.
- `DisjointnessOracle`: mines `owl:disjointWith` / `owl:AllDisjointClasses` / `owl:complementOf`,
  propagates through the materialised `subClassOf` closure, and provides train-time repulsion
  pairs plus a serve-time **answer-safe hard mask** (removes only *provably*-disjoint candidates)
  (`taxonomy.rs:527-560+`).

**(e) Graph fingerprint** (`src/fingerprint.rs`). Not a node vectoriser ‚Äî a **staleness guard**
binding a `.spqv`/`.spqg` artifact to the graph generation it was built against. Three fields
(`dict_len`, `triple_count`, 64-bit `content_hash`); the content hash folds **per-term lexical
FxHash values in sorted (dict-id-order-independent) order**, with fixed-width writes so the value
is architecture-independent (wasm32 == native) and thread-count-independent
(`fingerprint.rs:16-53, 124-157, 228-274`); a golden value is pinned (`fingerprint.rs:631-658`).
Deliberately an integrity check, not a MAC (`fingerprint.rs:55-59`). The load-bearing caveat: the
store is **keyed by dict id**, and the fingerprint is blind to a pure id permutation, so a store is
valid only against the exact persisted graph generation (`Graph::save`/`Graph::open`, never a
re-parse) ‚Äî a **term-keyed store is an acknowledged, unimplemented follow-up**
(`fingerprint.rs:61-90`, `src/store.rs:26-36`).

**(f) Deterministic preprocessing & sampling (P0)** (`src/structure.rs`).
`materialise_closure`/`close_for_vectorise` run the sparq-reason RDFS/OWL-RL closure *before*
vectorisation so entailed facts are real triples the encoders see (`structure.rs:98-122`);
`TypeConstraints::mine` reads declared+observed domain/range (`structure.rs:146-219`);
`NegativeSampler` draws corruptions from a SplitMix64 stream seeded per `(seed, triple, side)` ‚Äî
"reproducible across runs and platforms" (`structure.rs:281-391`), with determinism pinned by test
(`structure.rs:564-574`).

**(g) Deterministic renderers/readers**: `verbalize` produces the exact template string
`"<label>. a <type>. <description>"` deterministically from the graph (`src/verbalize.rs:145-203,
233-281`); `shacl_priors`/`provenance` are read-only prior extractors (¬ß4 below); `fuse_rrf`
/`fuse_scores` are pure rank-fusion arithmetic (`src/fuse.rs:1-25`).

### 1.2 Data-fitted (deterministic given pinned inputs, but corpus-dependent)

- `NumericEncoder::fit` / `DateEncoder::fit`: the normaliser depends on the **observed value set**
  per predicate (`encode.rs:258-271`). Same value + different fit corpus ‚Üí different code. For a
  canonical kernel this means the fit breakpoints must themselves be pinned/content-addressed.
- `ScalarQuantizer` / `ProductQuantizer` (`src/quant.rs:1-52`): SQ per-dim ranges and PQ k-means
  codebooks are learned from the stored vectors (compression, not semantics).

### 1.3 Trained / external components

- **`RemoteEmbedder`** (`embed.rs:104-253`): an OpenAI-compatible `/v1/embeddings` client ‚Äî the
  production text lane is an **external trained model**; the crate only validates the response
  (dim/finiteness/index, fail-closed, `embed.rs:401-453`). The `.spqv` header records **no model
  id, model version, or metric** ‚Äî an acknowledged gap
  (`research/specs/vector-genai-estate-recon.md`, "Key missing provenance").
- **`train.rs`** (`kge` feature): a minimal CPU DistMult/ComplEx trainer, deterministically seeded
  (SplitMix64 init + negatives, `train.rs:44-50`), but explicitly "research-grade, not a
  production training subsystem", existing only "to produce embeddings to measure"
  (`train.rs:9-19`).
- **`eval.rs`** (`kge` feature): a filtered link-prediction harness (MRR/Hits@k, 2√ó2 closure √ó
  type-negatives ablation, multi-seed, paired deltas) (`eval.rs:1-80`). **Checked as tasked: no
  benchmark numbers have been produced/committed since the design doc.** The module states "No
  numbers live in this file or any committed doc. The harness *computes* them at run time"
  (`eval.rs:73-80`); a repo grep finds no committed KGE ablation figures ‚Äî only literature numbers
  quoted with sources in `research/genai-kg-embeddings-vectorindex.md:86-101`. The design record's
  posture stands: "No benchmark numbers appear anywhere in this document ‚Äî none exist yet"
  (`research/structure-aware-vectorisation.md:8-12`).

**Bottom line for Q1.** The entire *literal/structural* lane stack ‚Äî router, thermometer numeric,
boolean sign, date, enum codebook, unit normaliser, taxonomy ancestor-bag/depth, hashed
sign-projection text fallback, schema header, fingerprint, closure preprocessing ‚Äî is
deterministic and training-free. The *relational/semantic* lane (what a node means beyond its
declared structure) is delegated to trained components (remote text embedder or the opt-in KGE
trainer). sparq's own design docs are candid that this split is deliberate
(`research/structure-aware-vectorisation.md:96-102`).

---

## 2. The decode direction: `grounding.rs`

`ground(graph, node, modality, cfg, introspection, store)` projects an **already-identified node**
into the object a consumer declared it needs (`src/grounding.rs:268-302`). Dispatch is by declared
`OutputType` ‚Üí `Modality` (`grounding.rs:118-145`); ambiguous intent defaults to the exact,
re-checkable subgraph (`grounding.rs:47-51, 129-131`). Four modalities (`grounding.rs:99-187`):

1. **`Subgraph`** ‚Üí `Vec<GroundFact>` ‚Äî `(predicate IRI, rendered object, is_literal)` triples in
   a **stable, dict-id-independent order** (sorted by predicate then object,
   `grounding.rs:386-427`). Minimality is ABSTAT-style: restrict to the predicates of the node's
   *effective (most-specific) type's* characteristic set, mined from `sparq-introspect`, with
   `rdf:type` always kept; fail-open to all facts when no pattern matches
   (`grounding.rs:434-506`).
2. **`TypedSubVector`** ‚Üí `{values: Vec<f32>, blocks: Vec<Encoder>}` ‚Äî slices the node's stored
   `.spqv` row to only the requested encoder families using the `SchemaHeader` block spans
   (`grounding.rs:736-761`). This is the "return only the numeric lane / only the taxonomy lane"
   projection.
3. **`NlString`** ‚Üí the deterministic `verbalize` passage, optionally extended with typed-value
   clauses (quantities, enum labels) under a char budget (`grounding.rs:310-349`).
4. **`TypedValue`** ‚Üí exact typed slots: `Boolean(bool)`, `Number{value, datatype}`,
   `Quantity{value, unit}` (recognising the QUDT `qudt:numericValue`+`qudt:unit` shape,
   `grounding.rs:628-647`), `Enum{member, label}` ‚Äî "each value is the literal's own value", never
   a cosine match (`grounding.rs:534-581`). Optional cross-unit reconciliation converts
   known-unit quantities to canonical SI, conservative on unknown/compound units
   (`grounding.rs:649-676`).

Completeness is **profile-relative by contract**: ground over a closure-materialised graph and the
result is complete relative to that entailment profile only ‚Äî "No answer-completeness claim is
made" (`grounding.rs:31-45, 252-257`).

**Two honesty notes for the kernel.** (i) This is *node ‚Üí object* projection, **not vector ‚Üí
node inversion**. The vector‚Üínode step in sparq is ANN search (`nearest_term`) followed by
grounding, and ‚Äî in the `neuro-symbolic` feature ‚Äî a **propose-then-verify** pipeline where a
deductive gate (SHACL + OWL inconsistency check) rejects candidates fail-closed
(`src/lib.rs:54-61`). (ii) Per-block decode exists only where the encoder is exactly invertible:
`BooleanEncoder::decode` (`encode.rs:380-387`) and `Codebook::decode` (`codebook.rs:123-133`)
round-trip; the thermometer numeric code has **no implemented decoder** (an inverse is
mathematically straightforward ‚Äî count saturated coordinates ‚Äî but is not in the code).

---

## 3. Where a `(concept-hash ‚Üí embedding)` table lands

The storage seam is exactly two files:

**`store.rs` ‚Äî the `.spqv` table itself.** One f32 row per **dict term id (u32)**, one flat mmap
file: `magic "SPQV" | version=2 | dim u32 | count u64 | reserved | 24-byte graph fingerprint |
dense [count √ó dim] f32 | sorted (id u32, slot u32) index` (`src/store.rs:4-17`). Lookup is one
binary search plus a contiguous aligned slice (`store.rs:38-43`). Interfaces:
`create(path, dim)` ‚Üí `put(id, &[f32])` ‚Üí `with_fingerprint(graph)` ‚Üí `finalize()`;
`open`/`open_from_bytes` for reads; `StreamingWriter` for O(1)-RAM builds (`store.rs:45-52,
163-226`); an optional delta sidecar (`.spqd`) for incremental add/remove/update behind the
`delta` feature (`store.rs:152-161`). The `SchemaHeader` travels as a sidecar byte string
(`encode.rs:538-543`).

**`import.rs` ‚Äî the bring-your-own-embeddings bulk path.** `ImportSpec { spqv_path, dim, ids,
binding }` plus either a NumPy `.npy` (v1‚Äìv3, 2-D, C-order, `<f4`/`<f8`) or a headerless flat f32
dump; **row `i` of the matrix is stored for `ids[i]`** ‚Äî the "row ‚Üí dict-id contract"
(`src/import.rs:13-35, 100-110`). Validation is fail-closed before any proportional allocation;
the read is streaming (peak memory O(dim + index)) (`import.rs:37-59`). `ImportBinding::Graph`
binds the fingerprint (`import.rs:80-92`).

**How the kernel's table maps onto this.** A concept hash is a `urn:concept:<multibase>` URN ‚Äî
an IRI. Interned into a sparq `Graph`, it gets a u32 dict id, and the whole estate (store, ANN,
filtered search, `vec:nearest`/`vec:search` SPARQL predicates, `src/lib.rs:110-123`) works
unchanged: build the graph of concept records, run the deterministic encoder out-of-process (or
in-process), and `import` the matrix against the interned ids. Two caveats:

1. **Id-keying vs hash-keying.** The `.spqv` is keyed by *mutable-generation* dict ids, with the
   staleness contract of ¬ß1.1(e). Concept hashes are immutable by construction, so the natural
   kernel key is the hash itself. sparq's own docs flag the term-keyed store as the right redesign
   (`fingerprint.rs:87-90`). Adapting `import.rs`'s row‚Üíid contract to a row‚Üímultihash contract
   (sorted multihash index instead of sorted u32 index) is a small format fork, not a rewrite.
2. **No model/encoder provenance in the header.** The concept-hash design (¬ß9a) wants tables
   "keyed by model identifier + version" as signed annotations; `.spqv` records neither
   (`research/specs/vector-genai-estate-recon.md`). For a *deterministic* encoder this becomes an
   encoder-version + profile-hash field ‚Äî which could live in the 12 reserved header bytes
   (`store.rs:11`) or the `SchemaHeader` sidecar.

# NSM‚ÄìUFO Bridge: Provisional Ontological Investigation

**Path:** `docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md`  
**Status:** PROVISIONAL design/interpretation note, 2026-07-11  
**Provenance:** transcribed 2026-07-11 from `poc/gpt56-review/nsm-ufo-bridge/last-message.json` (GPT-5.6 deliverable) per the review-gate finding that the named deliverable was absent; the source's lowercase epistemic tags are converted to the registry vocabulary (well-established ‚Üí MEASURED where repository-source-backed, LIT-BACKED where literature/spec-backed; plausible ‚Üí PROVISIONAL; speculative ‚Üí EXTRAPOLATION); content otherwise unchanged.  
**Scope:** feasibility of an NSM-derived concept-kernel ‚Üî foundational-ontology bridge. This document makes **no feasibility conclusion about the programme‚Äôs broader correctness or efficiency theses**.

Epistemic tags (registry vocabulary; all provisional):

- **[MEASURED]** ‚Äî directly supported by repository source or a mechanically checkable artifact.
- **[LIT-BACKED]** ‚Äî directly supported by a formal specification or established literature.
- **[PROVISIONAL]** ‚Äî a defensible design interpretation requiring validation.
- **[EXTRAPOLATION]** ‚Äî a proposed extension or empirical hypothesis; never a premise.

---

## 0. Executive answer

A bridge is possible, but not as a total one-to-one mapping and not as a reduction of all UFO categories to NSM primes.

The viable form is a **hybrid**:

1. The NSM-derived kernel remains the grounded, human-auditable **lexical/explicational layer**.
2. Selected prime constructions supply evidence for, or anchor, some foundational distinctions: entity, event, action, agent, time, place, parthood, causation, desire and goal.
3. UFO supplies a separate **formal ontological layer**: types versus individuals, endurants versus perdurants, identity criteria, rigidity, existential dependence, qualities and modes as dependent particulars, relators, participation, and intentional/social structures.
4. An authored and endorsed bridge record connects an explication to a UFO classification or axiom. It must be allowed to return **underdetermined**.
5. UFO-grounded classifications then compile into the OWL-RL, Horn and policy strata already proposed for the world-model/rules engine.

The resulting relationship is therefore:

```text
NSM explication  ‚îÄ‚îÄevidence/paraphrase‚îÄ‚îÄ‚ñ∂  bridge judgment
                                              ‚îÇ
                                     authored + endorsed
                                              ‚ñº
                                      UFO/OntoUML profile
                                              ‚îÇ
                                executable-fragment compilation
                                              ‚ñº
                             OWL-RL / Horn / validation / policy
                                              ‚îÇ
                                              ‚ñº
                                  proof-carrying rules engine
```

The bridge provides part of the hoped-for ‚Äúbest of both worlds,‚Äù but only if ‚Äúbest of both worlds‚Äù means **two cooperating foundations**. It does not show that UFO‚Äôs formal machinery is secretly reducible to the 65 primes.

**Recommendation:** pursue the hybrid. Treat direct mapping as a sparse alignment, build-up as explanatory evidence, and formal UFO commitments as an explicit sidecar with separate provenance. Do not infer UFO `Kind`, `Role`, `Phase`, domain/range, rigidity, or identity criteria automatically from the occurrence of NSM `KIND`, argument roles, or ordinary-language class descriptions.

## 1. Inventory: the Kernel-of-Truth side

### 1.1 Exact profile-1 prime inventory

The encoder‚Äôs source of truth is [`encoder/src/lexicon.ts`](../../../encoder/src/lexicon.ts). It declares the NSM chart as `v20-2022`, fixes chart order as codebook-significant, and contains these exact 65 records:

| Category | Exact profile-1 exponents |
|---|---|
| Substantives | `I`, `YOU`, `SOMEONE`, `SOMETHING~THING`, `PEOPLE`, `BODY` |
| Relational substantives | `KIND`, `PART` |
| Determiners | `THIS`, `THE-SAME`, `OTHER~ELSE~ANOTHER` |
| Quantifiers | `ONE`, `TWO`, `SOME`, `ALL`, `MUCH~MANY`, `LITTLE~FEW` |
| Evaluators | `GOOD`, `BAD` |
| Descriptors | `BIG`, `SMALL` |
| Mental predicates | `THINK`, `KNOW`, `WANT`, `DON'T-WANT`, `FEEL`, `SEE`, `HEAR` |
| Speech | `SAY`, `WORDS`, `TRUE` |
| Actions, events, movement | `DO`, `HAPPEN`, `MOVE` |
| Location, existence, specification, possession | `BE-SOMEWHERE`, `THERE-IS`, `BE-SPEC`, `IS-MINE` |
| Life and death | `LIVE`, `DIE` |
| Time | `WHEN~TIME`, `NOW`, `BEFORE`, `AFTER`, `A-LONG-TIME`, `A-SHORT-TIME`, `FOR-SOME-TIME`, `MOMENT` |
| Space | `WHERE~PLACE`, `HERE`, `ABOVE`, `BELOW`, `FAR`, `NEAR`, `SIDE`, `INSIDE`, `TOUCH` |
| Logical concepts | `NOT`, `MAYBE`, `CAN`, `BECAUSE`, `IF` |
| Intensifier/augmentor | `VERY`, `MORE` |
| Similarity | `LIKE~AS~WAY` |

This broadly matches the stabilized contemporary NSM inventory. In particular, the repository correctly uses `IS-MINE`, rather than a generic possession prime `HAVE`; Wierzbicka describes the replacement of `HAVE` by the egocentric `MINE/BE MINE` form. The contemporary debate also commonly renders `PART` as the converse construction `HAVE PARTS`. **[LIT-BACKED]** ([Wierzbicka 2021](https://journals.rudn.ru/linguistics/article/view/26795))

### 1.2 The kernel is more than 65 labels

The bridge must target the complete profile-1 AST, not a bag of primes. The encoder adds a closed compositional metalanguage:

- 13 core roles: `agent`, `undergoer`, `experiencer`, `stimulus`, `addressee`, `topic`, `quote`, `complement`, `attribute`, `locus`, `possessor`, `instrument`, `comitative`.
- Four adjunct roles: `time`, `duration`, `place`, `manner`.
- Nineteen predicate frames, covering action/event, mental, speech, location/existence/specification/possession, life and death.
- Eleven operators: `NOT`, `CAN`, `MAYBE`, `IF`, `BECAUSE`, `WHEN`, `LIKE`, `AFTER`, `BEFORE`, `VERY`, `MORE`.
- Three top-level frames: `InstanceSchema`, `WhenTrue`, `RelationalSchema`.
- Five referent kinds: `SomeoneRef`, `SomethingRef`, `TimeRef`, `PlaceRef`, `ClauseRef`.
- Substantive phrases of the form `[determiner] [quantifier] [modifier]* head [restriction]`, with binding and coreference.
- Embedded clauses, quotations with re-anchored indexicals, temporal anchors and recursively resolved concept references.
- Caps of 32 clauses, 32 referents and structural depth 12.

This machinery is semantically important. For example, an event interpretation depends on the difference between `HAPPEN` as predicate, a clause referent, and an explication whose head denotes an event type. Agentivity depends on `DO` plus its `agent` role, not on the isolated `DO` vector. **[MEASURED]**

The deterministic vector is an encoding of this explication structure, with role/filler binding, ordered clauses and recursive concept references. It does not itself add ontological truth beyond the AST. **[MEASURED]** See [`docs/architecture.md`](../../architecture.md) and [`reports/deterministic-concept-vectors.md`](../../../reports/deterministic-concept-vectors.md).

### 1.3 Scientific posture

The repository already adopts NSM for its engineering properties‚Äîa small, closed, human-auditable vocabulary and grammar‚Äînot as a settled proof of psychological universality or uniquely correct conceptual decomposition. Explications are treated as stipulative, endorsed artifacts. **[MEASURED]**

That posture should carry into the bridge. A bridge record says:

> ‚ÄúGiven this endorsed explication and these additional modeling commitments, classify the concept this way.‚Äù

It should not say:

> ‚ÄúThe prime text mechanically discovers the one true ontological category.‚Äù

## 2. Inventory: the UFO side

### 2.1 UFO-A: endurants and type structure

UFO‚Äôs primary distinction is between:

- **Endurants**: individuals existing in time with all their parts and capable of qualitative change while retaining numerical identity.
- **Perdurants/events**: individuals unfolding in time by accumulating temporal parts.

A unique `Kind` supplies the identity principle governing which changes an endurant can undergo while remaining the same individual. UFO distinguishes types and individuals explicitly and formalizes instantiation and specialization in first-order modal logic. **[LIT-BACKED]** ([Guizzardi et al., ‚ÄúUFO: Unified Foundational Ontology‚Äù](https://nemo.inf.ufes.br/wp-content/uploads/ufo_unified_foundational_ontology_2021.pdf))

Important UFO-A categories include:

- `ObjectKind`, `CollectiveKind`, `QuantityKind`
- `Subkind`
- `Role`, `RoleMixin`
- `Phase`, `PhaseMixin`
- `Category`, `Mixin`
- `QualityKind`
- `ModeKind`, including dispositions/capabilities
- `RelatorKind`
- qualities, modes and relators as dependent particulars
- formal, material, mediation and characterization relations
- identity, rigidity, sortality and dependence meta-properties
- extensional mereology, supplemented by more specific functional-part relations

`Kind` in UFO is rigid and identity-supplying. `Role` is anti-rigid and relationally conditioned. `Phase` is anti-rigid and intrinsically conditioned. A `Relator` is a reified, existentially dependent relational complex mediating multiple individuals. These are not merely labels for ordinary-language ‚Äúkind,‚Äù ‚Äúrole,‚Äù ‚Äústate‚Äù or ‚Äúrelationship.‚Äù **[LIT-BACKED]**

### 2.2 UFO-B: events and participation

UFO-B covers:

- events/perdurants
- event and temporal mereology
- temporal ordering
- change and causation
- object participation in events
- the relation between endurant dispositions and their event manifestations
- atomic and complex events and, in fuller UFO work, processes

An endurant participates in a perdurant through a manifestation of a disposition inhering in that endurant. Thus participation is more formally committed than merely appearing as a grammatical argument of `HAPPEN`. **[LIT-BACKED]** ([UFO overview](https://www.inf.ufes.br/~gguizzardi/UFO-Story.pdf))

### 2.3 UFO-C: intentional and social entities

UFO-C builds on UFO-A and UFO-B and covers:

- agents and non-agentive objects
- beliefs, desires and intentions
- propositional contents and goals
- intentional actions
- social commitments and corresponding claims
- social roles and normative descriptions
- social relators

An agent can bear intentional moments and perform intentional actions. A goal is the propositional content of an intention. A social commitment inheres in one agent, depends externally on another, and is paired with a claim; such pairs can compose a social relator. **[LIT-BACKED]** ([Bringuente, Falbo and Guizzardi 2011](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/220541502_Using_a_Foundational_Ontology_for_Reengineering_a_Software_Process_Ontology))

### 2.4 BFO and DOLCE analogues

The bridge pattern is not UFO-specific:

| UFO | DOLCE analogue | BFO analogue |
|---|---|---|
| Endurant | Endurant/object | Continuant |
| Perdurant/event | Perdurant/event; state/process/accomplishment/achievement | Occurrent/process |
| Object | Physical/non-physical endurant | Independent continuant/material entity/object |
| Quality | Individual quality and quale/quality region | Specifically dependent continuant: quality |
| Mode/disposition | Non-qualitative dependent property; DOLCE extensions vary | Realizable entity: disposition/function |
| Role | Social/functional role, often via DnS extensions | Realizable entity: role |
| Participation | Participation relation | Participation/has participant |
| Time/space | Temporal and spatial qualities/regions | Temporal, spatial and spatiotemporal regions |

DOLCE is linguistically and cognitively oriented and therefore a natural secondary comparison target. BFO is a useful interoperability target for the programme‚Äôs OBO assets. UFO is the best primary target here because it supplies the richest formal account of `Kind`, `Role`, `Phase`, relators and intentional/social entities. **[PROVISIONAL]** ([DOLCE overview](https://www.loa.istc.cnr.it/index.php/dolce/); [BFO 2020](https://bfo-ontology.github.io/bfo-2020.html))

## 3. Direction 1: direct mapping

### 3.1 What ‚Äúmapping‚Äù can mean

A prime and a foundational category live at different levels:

- An NSM prime is an elementary meaning used in linguistic explications.
- A UFO category classifies entities or types and is constrained by a formal theory.
- Some primes are predicates or operators rather than entity categories.
- Some UFO notions are meta-properties of types, not ordinary first-order concepts.

Accordingly, the table uses:

- **A ‚Äî strong anchor:** a reliable bridge starting point, but not necessarily logical equivalence.
- **P ‚Äî partial/contextual:** overlap exists, but additional disambiguation or commitments are required.
- **√ò ‚Äî no direct category mapping:** the prime belongs to logic, discourse or lexical semantics rather than a UFO base category.

### 3.2 Correspondence table 1: primes to UFO

| Kernel primes | Closest UFO target | Status | Analysis |
|---|---|---:|---|

| `SOMEONE` | Human agent/person | P | `SOMEONE` supplies personhood in NSM, while UFO `Agent` includes physical and social agents and is defined through intentional moments and actions. It is narrower in one respect and broader in another. |

## 0. The one distinction everything below depends on

"Not expressible with the semantic-web stack" is three different claims, and the maintainer's
justification requirement is only dischargeable if every gap statement says WHICH one it is:

- **Axis 1 ‚Äî not STATABLE.** No OWL 2 DL / RDF / N3 encoding of the proposition exists at all
  (e.g. an induction schema, a propositional-attitude operator). This is the strong claim.
- **Axis 2 ‚Äî statable but not DERIVABLE in the tractable profile.** OWL 2 DL can state it and a
  tableau reasoner can conclude from it, but the rule-based profiles (OWL 2 RL, N3 Horn,
  Datalog) provably cannot, because their rule heads are assertion-only and they perform no
  case analysis. R-COVER-ELIM is the measured house example [MEASURED: sparq
  `inference-conformance-report.md` PR1 divergences; CV-8 in the WMRE-1 fold-in].
- **Axis 3 ‚Äî statable but with the WRONG SEMANTICS.** The stack can write something
  syntactically adjacent, but its open-world, monotone, no-unique-names model theory makes the
  written thing mean something else (closed-world counting, defaults, negation-as-failure,
  integrity constraints).

The existing WMRE-1 regime discipline (`regime ‚àà {owl-rl, horn-def, policy}` on every rule and
proof node, PROPOSED-ASM-1162) is the axis-2/axis-3 machinery already in place: the *policy*
regime is precisely "we pre-compiled an axis-2 case elimination into a Horn rule whose
extra-logical premises (covering, UNA) are named ASMs". This analysis extends that discipline
into a general gap taxonomy (¬ß4.1) rather than inventing a new one.

## 1. What the semantic-web stack CAN and CANNOT express

### 1.1 The layers and what each buys

| Layer | Expressive content | Reasoning cost | In-estate status |
|---|---|---|---|
| RDF | Ground **binary** atoms (triples); blank nodes = existential variables in data | ‚Äî | native (`urn:kot:` IRIs) |
| RDFS | subClassOf / subPropertyOf hierarchies, domain/range typing | PTIME, trivial rules | sparq-reason, conformance-tested |
| OWL 2 DL (SROIQ(D)) | Boolean class constructors incl. **classical negation and disjunction**, existential/universal restrictions, qualified cardinality, inverse/functional/(ir)reflexive/disjoint roles, **regular** property chains, nominals, datatypes with facets | N2EXPTIME-complete worst case [LIT-BACKED: Horrocks/Kutz/Sattler 2006; Kazakov 2008]; practical via tableau/hypertableau (HermiT, Openllet) | NOT in estate; no Rust-class engine |
| OWL 2 RL | The rule-implementable subset: assertion-only heads, no disjunction elimination, no existential-head individual invention | PTIME, forward rules | **primary** (sparq-reason OwlRl; twin) |
| OWL 2 EL | Existentials + chains, no inverse/universal/cardinality; classifies SNOMED-scale | PTIME (ELK) | not needed yet; RO shard appears RL-compatible by kind (profile-validation pending, ¬ß4.3) |
| OWL 2 QL | Query-rewriting fragment | AC‚Å∞ data complexity | not needed |
| SPARQL 1.1 | **Queries**: arbitrary graph patterns (full FO-query power over the data), aggregation (COUNT/SUM/AVG), arithmetic in FILTER/BIND, property paths, **negation-as-failure** (NOT EXISTS / MINUS) | PSPACE-complete eval; but it is a query language ‚Äî closed-world at query time, no entailment | sparq (query side) |
| SHACL (+AF) | **Closed-world integrity constraints** over shapes; SHACL-AF adds rules | validation, not entailment | adopted as separate artifact per ASM-1162 rider (i) |
| N3 | Horn rules over triples with **cyclic rule graphs** (beyond OWL's regularity restriction), builtins (math:, string:, list:), graph quoting, **scoped NAF** (`log:notIncludes`) | terminating only if the ruleset is safe; undecidable in general | sparq-reason `reason_n3`, the RULES-1 `rules.n3` carrier |
| SWRL (DL-safe) | Horn rules over OWL atoms, builtins; monotone, no NAF, no head disjunction | decidable only DL-safe [LIT-BACKED: Motik et al. 2005] | subsumed by N3 here |

### 1.2 What the stack, taken as a whole, CANNOT do

Each row states the gap, the axis, and the one-line justification the maintainer's directive
requires. These are properties of the formalisms, not of our engine [LIT-BACKED throughout:
W3C OWL 2 Profiles; OWL 2 Direct Semantics; N3 CG spec 2023; standard DL literature].

| # | Gap | Axis | Why the stack cannot do it (the justification) |
|---|---|---|---|
| G1 | **Full first-order quantification** (arbitrary ‚àÄ/‚àÉ alternation over ‚â•3 variables; non-tree-shaped axioms) | 1 | OWL 2 DL is (approximately) the two-variable + counting + regular-role fragment of FOL, chosen to keep satisfiability decidable; an axiom whose variable graph is not tree-shaped (beyond what regular role chains linearise) has no SROIQ encoding. |
| G2 | **Case elimination / reasoning by disjunction** in the RULE profiles (our R-COVER-ELIM) | 2 | OWL 2 RL rule heads assert single atoms; the profile is documented incomplete for entailments that require splitting on `A ‚äî B` ‚Äî the covering+functional+differentFrom‚Üífather inference is *statable* in OWL 2 DL and derivable by tableau, but no RL/N3/Datalog engine can perform the split [MEASURED: sparq conformance PR1; reproduced independently by GPT-5.6, CV-8]. |
| G3 | **Negation-as-failure / closed-world defaults** as entailment ("not known to be X", "birds fly unless...") | 3 | OWL model theory is open-world and monotone: absence of a triple is not evidence, and no OWL axiom can conclude from absence; SPARQL/N3 NAF exists but is non-monotonic and sits *outside* the OWL entailment relation (query/scoped semantics, not ontology semantics). |
| G4 | **Unique names** | 3 | OWL makes no unique-name assumption; distinctness must be asserted pairwise (`owl:differentFrom`, quadratic) or via `owl:AllDifferent` ‚Äî which is why UNA is a named, item-scoped stipulation here (ASM-1120), not a free ambient fact. |
| G5 | **Arithmetic over values; comparisons between derived quantities** ("BMI = kg/m¬≤", "more children than siblings") | 1 (OWL) / 3 (N3, SPARQL) | OWL datatypes admit only facet restrictions on single values ‚Äî there is no term algebra, no function symbols, no cross-value computation; N3 `math:` builtins and SPARQL BIND compute, but procedurally, outside the model theory, with no completeness story. |
| G6 | **Counting/aggregation as a class definition** ("family with exactly 3 recorded children" as derived from data) | 3 | OWL qualified cardinality constrains *models*, it does not count *data*: open-world semantics means unstated children may exist, so no OWL axiom can conclude "exactly 3" from three assertions; aggregation needs CWA (SPARQL aggregates, ASP `#count`) ‚Äî query semantics, not entailment. |
| G7 | **n-ary relations and events** ("x gave y to z at t") | 1 | The RDF data model is binary; n-ary facts require reification patterns, and OWL axioms cannot then quantify smoothly across a reified event's participant slots (role chains do not traverse reification shapes in general). |
| G8 | **Function symbols / terms** (f(x) inside axioms; term rewriting) | 1 | OWL/N3 have no term constructors; skolem functions appear only implicitly via existentials, which the RL profile refuses to instantiate (no individual invention ‚Äî ASM-1162 rider ii). |
| G9 | **Higher-order statements** ("every relation with property P is transitive"; axiom schemas) | 1 | OWL 2 punning is syntactic only; the direct semantics never quantifies over classes or properties, so schema-level generalisations must be expanded per instance by a compiler, not stated once. |
| G10 | **Temporal and modal operators** (before/after as operators, "necessarily", "believes/wants/knows that œÜ") | 1 | OWL/RDF have no modalities and no propositional arguments; time must be reified (4D fluents [LIT-BACKED: Welty & Fikes 2006]) at combinatorial cost, and interval-algebra composition (Allen) has disjunctive entries ‚Äî outside Horn (compounds with G2). |
| G11 | **Induction, recursion beyond transitive closure, mathematics** | 1 | Transitive/chain closure is the *only* fixed point OWL offers; an induction schema is second-order (or an infinite FO axiom family), and arithmetic with induction is not merely outside OWL but outside every complete decidable logic (G√∂del) ‚Äî this is the maintainer's #19 mathematics case, permanently un-encodable in this stack. |
| G12 | **Integrity constraints as constraints** ("every kot-world person record MUST carry a gender field") | 3 | An OWL axiom read open-world *infers* rather than *checks* (a missing field triggers inference or silence, never an error); constraint semantics needs SHACL ‚Äî which the architecture already emits as a separate validation artifact, never as entailment (ASM-1162 rider i). |

### 1.3 What the stack CAN do that is easy to underestimate

For balance ‚Äî the following are fully in-stack, and several were load-bearing in the RULES-1

## 2. Audit of OUR concept set: where OWL/N3 suffices and where it genuinely falls short

### 2.1 Kinship vertical (RULES-1) ‚Äî SUFFICIENT, with one named axis-2 patch

Everything in the frozen-inventory kinship vertical is in-stack: R-SUBP, R-DOM/R-RNG, R-INV,
R-CHAIN are OWL 2 RL; the single non-RL item, R-COVER-ELIM, is **axis 2, not axis 1** ‚Äî the
covering inference is statable in OWL 2 DL (`parent ‚äë mother ‚äî father` + functional mother +
differentFrom entails father under tableau), and its non-derivability is a property of the RL
profile (assertion-only heads, no disjunction elimination) [MEASURED: sparq conformance PR1].
The standing remedy ‚Äî pre-compile the case analysis into one monotone Horn *policy* rule whose
covering (ASM-1121) and UNA (ASM-1120) premises are named, registered stipulations visible in
every `why()` proof ‚Äî is an **in-stack** answer at the N3 layer with the epistemics disclosed.
The alternative (adopt a full-DL tableau engine to get the inference "natively") buys nothing
we need at the cost of N2EXPTIME machinery, a Java stack, weaker proof-tree/fail-closed
discipline, and the loss of the ¬µs-scale certificate loop. ‚Üí PROPOSED-ASM-1232.

### 2.2 g2's claim families (R1 subClassOf, R3 domain/range, R4 existentials) ‚Äî EXPRESSIVITY IS NOT THE BINDING CONSTRAINT

All three families are OWL-EL-grade statable: subsumptions, sortal typing, and
`‚äë ‚àÉrole.Class` existentials. What failed at proxy strength on g2 was not the ability to
*state* these claims but their ordinary-meaning **soundness** ‚Äî the blind judge pair endorsed
only ~29‚Äì45 % (bracket), with the collapse concentrated in the R3 domain/range renderings
(domain 0/21 concordant-yes) [MEASURED at LLM-proxy strength: `docs/next/interpretations/g2.md`;
PROVISIONAL-ON-LLM-PROXY; human gold adjudicates]. No richer logic fixes a wrong sortal claim ‚Äî
a false statement in FOL is exactly as false as in OWL. Expressivity work must therefore never
be cited as a remedy on the g2/ŒÝ line. One genuine expressivity note does attach to R4: OWL
states `person ‚äë ‚àÉmother.person` but the RL engine rightly refuses to *use* it to mint an
anonymous mother (G8/ASM-1162 rider ii); deriving named facts from existentials is a real
axis-2 boundary we have chosen to keep closed (refusal over skolemisation). ‚Üí PROPOSED-ASM-1238.

### 2.3 NSM-style explications ‚Äî GENUINELY OUTSIDE (axis 1), and already handled correctly

The kernel's native formalism is NSM explication over the 65-prime lexicon. The primes include
propositional-attitude and modal/temporal/conditional operators as first-class citizens:
THINK, KNOW, WANT, FEEL, SAY (attitudes taking *propositional* arguments), IF, BECAUSE, MAYBE,
CAN, NOT, WHEN/BEFORE/AFTER, GOOD/BAD. A typical explication is a quantified, tensed,
counterfactual mini-text ("X did something; because of this, Y thinks: 'I don't want this'").
**Justification (axis 1, gaps G10 + G7 + G1):** OWL/RDF have no operators over propositions ‚Äî
`wants(x, œÜ)` where œÜ is itself a quantified formula has no SROIQ encoding (DL is a fragment
of non-modal, non-intensional FOL); reifying œÜ as a node loses its logical structure for
inference. This is why C1 compiles *authored, endorsed constraint records* (functional,
domain/range, disjoint, subPropertyOf, coveredBy, chains) and never attempts wholesale
NL‚ÜíOWL translation of explications ‚Äî the architecture's existing stance (ASM-1126/CV-2) is
hereby recorded as an *expressivity* necessity, not merely a prudence choice: the full content
of an explication admits **no semantics-preserving encoding into the currently adopted
extensional entailment regimes** (its *syntax* can of course be carried as RDF data or quoted
N3 formulae, but the embedded formula's logical structure is then opaque to those regimes'
entailment); only its extensional shadow enters the entailment layer.
‚Üí PROPOSED-ASM-1233.

### 2.4 Mathematics / the Lean cases (maintainer #19) ‚Äî GENUINELY OUTSIDE (axis 1, permanent)

The estate's Lean corpora (`data/math-lean-sample/`, `data/mathlib-1000-sample/`,
`data/math-v0/`, `data/math-mm/`) contain induction proofs, dependent types, and higher-order
statements. **Justification (G11 + G9 + G5):** induction schemas are second-order; arithmetic
with induction admits no complete decidable axiomatisation (G√∂del), so it is outside every OWL
profile *by mathematical necessity, not engineering choice*; and Mathlib-grade statements
quantify over types and functions (higher-order), which OWL's direct semantics never does.
The already-registered Lean seam (PROPOSED-ASM-1196: endorsed formal content compiling to a
proof-assistant backend at the same authored C1 boundary, returning regime-tagged
proof-carrying derivations) is the correct escape hatch; this analysis adds only the regime
name (`proof-assistant`) and one cheap near-term use: proving, once, inside Lean, that
R-COVER-ELIM is sound given its covering/functional/UNA premises ‚Äî machine-checking the ¬ß3
soundness argument of WMRE-1. ‚Üí PROPOSED-ASM-1234.

### 2.5 World-model needs on the visible horizon ‚Äî MIXED, each named

- **Temporal kinship/narrative** ("who was x's spouse *before* t"; CLUTRR stories have event
  order): axis 1 (G10, G7). Binary triples cannot index a relation by time without reification,
  and Allen-composition reasoning is disjunctive (re-enters G2). In-stack mitigation exists
  (fluent reification + Horn rules over explicit time points) and should be tried first; a
  genuine interval-algebra need would justify ASP.
- **Closed-world counting** ("only child", "has exactly 3 recorded children"): axis 3 (G6, G3).
  Open-world OWL cannot conclude from absence; SPARQL aggregation answers the *query* form
  in-stack; deriving *facts* from counts needs ASP/Datalog-with-aggregates.
- **Defaults/exceptions** ("legal parent = biological parent unless adoption stated"): axis 3
  (G3). Monotone logics cannot retract; this is default logic / stable-model territory.
- **Schema-level governance** ("every axiom record must cite an endorsing explication sha"):
  axis 1 (G9) ‚Äî but correctly handled *procedurally* (validators, mint pipeline), and should
  stay procedural; no logic upgrade warranted.

### 2.6 The gUFO datum (maintainer's own observation, generalised)

The maintainer notes only gUFO ‚Äî the lightweight subset ‚Äî of UFO is modelled in RDF. That is
not an accident of effort: full UFO is formalised in quantified **modal** logic, and its
load-bearing metaproperties are modal through and through ‚Äî rigidity ("a Kind applies to its
instances in every world in which they exist"), anti-rigidity (Phases/Roles), relational
dependence [LIT-BACKED: Guizzardi 2005; gUFO spec, Almeida et al.]. **Justification (axis 1,
G10 + G9):** rigidity quantifies over possible worlds and over predicates; OWL has neither
modality nor predicate quantification, so gUFO necessarily demotes rigidity to annotation-level
discipline (naming conventions, disjointness patterns) that a reasoner cannot enforce. The
general lesson for us: when a foundational ontology's *inference-bearing* content is modal,
the RDF rendering keeps the taxonomy and loses the theorems ‚Äî exactly the failure mode our
authored-shadow C1 stance avoids by never pretending the shadow is the explication.
‚Üí PROPOSED-ASM-1237.

## 4. Recommendation: a layered stance with a machine-readable justification gate

### 4.1 The gap taxonomy (the justification vocabulary)

Register the gap codes so every future "we need more expressivity" claim must pick one and
name its axis: **GAP-CASE** (axis-2 disjunction elimination, G2), **GAP-NAF** (closed-world/
defaults, G3), **GAP-UNA** (G4), **GAP-ARITH** (G5), **GAP-AGG** (G6), **GAP-NARY** (G7),
**GAP-FUNC** (G8), **GAP-HO** (G9), **GAP-MODAL** (attitudes/rigidity/time-as-operator, G10),
**GAP-MATH** (induction/arithmetic-with-proof, G11), **GAP-CONSTRAINT** (G12). A proposal that
cannot name its gap code has not met the maintainer's justification bar. ‚Üí PROPOSED-ASM-1230.

### 4.2 The ladder (innermost layer that suffices, always)

| Layer | Formalism | Regime tag | When | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| L-A | OWL 2 RL (sparq-reason OwlRl + twin) | `owl-rl` | default for all TBox/ABox entailment | live (RULES-1) |
| L-B | Safe N3/Datalog Horn rules (sparq `reason_n3`) | `horn-def` | cyclic rule graphs OWL cannot state (sibling/cousin diamonds), guarded inequality bodies | live carrier; regime unused so far (disclosed in RESULT.md) |
| L-C | Pre-compiled case eliminations with named ASM premises | `policy` | finite, enumerable case splits (GAP-CASE) whose covering/UNA premises can be endorsed | live (R-COVER-ELIM) |
| L-D | **ASP (clingo)** ‚Äî the designated first step OUTSIDE the stack | `stable-model` (new) | GAP-NAF, GAP-AGG, GAP-UNA-at-scale, and any GAP-CASE that is not finitely pre-compilable | NOT adopted; gated on a named experiment with a concrete blocked inference |
| L-E | **Lean 4** ‚Äî the proof-assistant seam (ASM-1196) | `proof-assistant` (new) | GAP-MATH, GAP-HO; plus one-off soundness certificates for L-C rules | registered future work, out of RULES-1 scope |
| (L-F) | Full FOL (CL/SUO-KIF via E/Vampire) | `fol-atp` (reserved) | only if the onto-sumo vertical's translation bead shows content that is FOL-statable but neither Horn-compilable (L-B/C) nor worth Lean cost (L-E) | reserved; not scheduled |

Rules of the ladder: (i) every rule/derivation carries its layer's regime tag end-to-end
(extends ASM-1162); (ii) a rule may sit at layer N only with a recorded justification naming
the gap code and axis that excludes every layer < N; (iii) L-D/L-E outputs never silently mix
into `owl-rl` closures ‚Äî cross-regime derivations are visibly composite in `why()` trees;
(iv) fail-closed refusal (`ERR_RULE_UNIMPLEMENTED`) remains the answer for content no
adopted layer covers ‚Äî approximation is never the answer. ‚Üí PROPOSED-ASM-1231, 1235.

### 4.3 What this changes for RULES-1's engine choice: NOTHING

The audit in ¬ß2 finds no concept in RULES-1's frozen inventory, in the g2 claim families, or
in the OBO RO second vertical that requires L-D or beyond: the kinship vertical is L-A + one
L-C rule (measured working: certificate C_dec 1.0 stated / 0.0-reproducible entailed, engine
0.9955 Wilson-LB on third-party gold [MEASURED: `poc/rules-1/RESULT.md`; the certificate rerun
is now pinned by the FROZEN `registry/experiments/rules-1.json`, frozen 2026-07-11 ‚Äî no longer
"exploratory pre-freeze"]); g2's families are EL-statable and their problem is soundness, not
expressivity (¬ß2.2); the RO shard *appears* RL-compatible on relation-kind inspection
(`inverse_of`, `transitive_over`, `holds_over_chain`), but RL conformance depends on syntactic
placement and global restrictions, not on the presence of familiar axiom kinds [LIT-BACKED:
W3C OWL 2 Profiles, https://www.w3.org/TR/owl2-profiles/] ‚Äî an actual OWL 2 RL
profile-validation run over the shard is required before "RL-profile material" is asserted as
MEASURED. **Keep sparq-reason + the
differential twin exactly as frozen; RULES-1's arms, endpoints, kill rules, and envelope are
untouched by this analysis.** The layered stance changes only what we *say* (justifications,
regime tags, the two reserved regime names) and what we *plan* (ASP experiment gate, Lean
seam sequencing). ‚Üí PROPOSED-ASM-1236.

### 4.4 Sequencing (recommendation only; maintainer decides)

1. Register the taxonomy + ladder (this document's ASMs) ‚Äî $0, pure bookkeeping.
2. RULES-1 proceeds unchanged.
3. First L-D candidate, *when a concrete blocked inference exists* (e.g. closed-world
   counting or a temporal case in a later world): a small pre-registered ASP twin experiment
   at the same certificate discipline. Not before.
4. Lean seam per ASM-1196 timing (much later), with the R-COVER-ELIM soundness certificate as
   the cheap first Lean artifact if the seam opens.
5. onto-sumo translation bead decides L-F's fate; nothing else waits on it.

docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:111:## 2. Inventory: the UFO side
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:113:### 2.1 UFO-A: endurants and type structure
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:115:UFO‚Äôs primary distinction is between:
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:117:- **Endurants**: individuals existing in time with all their parts and capable of qualitative change while retaining numerical identity.
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:120:A unique `Kind` supplies the identity principle governing which changes an endurant can undergo while remaining the same individual. UFO distinguishes types and individuals explicitly and formalizes instantiation and specialization in first-order modal logic. **[LIT-BACKED]** ([Guizzardi et al., ‚ÄúUFO: Unified Foundational Ontology‚Äù](https://nemo.inf.ufes.br/wp-content/uploads/ufo_unified_foundational_ontology_2021.pdf))
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:122:Important UFO-A categories include:
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:124:- `ObjectKind`, `CollectiveKind`, `QuantityKind`
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:125:- `Subkind`
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:126:- `Role`, `RoleMixin`
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:127:- `Phase`, `PhaseMixin`
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:128:- `Category`, `Mixin`
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:129:- `QualityKind`
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:130:- `ModeKind`, including dispositions/capabilities
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:131:- `RelatorKind`
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:134:- identity, rigidity, sortality and dependence meta-properties
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:137:`Kind` in UFO is rigid and identity-supplying. `Role` is anti-rigid and relationally conditioned. `Phase` is anti-rigid and intrinsically conditioned. A `Relator` is a reified, existentially dependent relational complex mediating multiple individuals. These are not merely labels for ordinary-language ‚Äúkind,‚Äù ‚Äúrole,‚Äù ‚Äústate‚Äù or ‚Äúrelationship.‚Äù **[LIT-BACKED]**
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:139:### 2.2 UFO-B: events and participation
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:141:UFO-B covers:
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:149:- atomic and complex events and, in fuller UFO work, processes
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:151:An endurant participates in a perdurant through a manifestation of a disposition inhering in that endurant. Thus participation is more formally committed than merely appearing as a grammatical argument of `HAPPEN`. **[LIT-BACKED]** ([UFO overview](https://www.inf.ufes.br/~gguizzardi/UFO-Story.pdf))
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:153:### 2.3 UFO-C: intentional and social entities
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:155:UFO-C builds on UFO-A and UFO-B and covers:
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:162:- social roles and normative descriptions
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:169:The bridge pattern is not UFO-specific:
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:171:| UFO | DOLCE analogue | BFO analogue |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:178:| Role | Social/functional role, often via DnS extensions | Realizable entity: role |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:182:DOLCE is linguistically and cognitively oriented and therefore a natural secondary comparison target. BFO is a useful interoperability target for the programme‚Äôs OBO assets. UFO is the best primary target here because it supplies the richest formal account of `Kind`, `Role`, `Phase`, relators and intentional/social entities. **[PROVISIONAL]** ([DOLCE overview](https://www.loa.istc.cnr.it/index.php/dolce/); [BFO 2020](https://bfo-ontology.github.io/bfo-2020.html))
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:190:A prime and a foundational category live at different levels:
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:193:- A UFO category classifies entities or types and is constrained by a formal theory.
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:195:- Some UFO notions are meta-properties of types, not ordinary first-order concepts.
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:201:- **√ò ‚Äî no direct category mapping:** the prime belongs to logic, discourse or lexical semantics rather than a UFO base category.
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:203:### 3.2 Correspondence table 1: primes to UFO
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:205:| Kernel primes | Closest UFO target | Status | Analysis |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:207:| `I`, `YOU` | Particular agent/person in a discourse role | P | Indexical reference identifies speaker/addressee. UFO supplies no universal category ‚ÄúI‚Äù; the referent may be modeled as a human agent playing a conversational role. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:208:| `SOMEONE` | Human agent/person | P | `SOMEONE` supplies personhood in NSM, while UFO `Agent` includes physical and social agents and is defined through intentional moments and actions. It is narrower in one respect and broader in another. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:209:| `PEOPLE` | Collection or plurality of human agents | P | May denote several persons, a collective, or people generically. UFO distinguishes plural quantification, collectives and social agents; NSM `PEOPLE` does not decide among them. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:210:| `SOMETHING~THING` | Entity, individual, object or endurant | P | It is a general non-person substantive, not an exact synonym for UFO `Object` or `Endurant`. It does not by itself distinguish enduring things, events, qualities, relators or abstract entities. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:211:| `BODY` | Physical object / biological endurant | A/P | A strong anchor for a physical endurant, but UFO still requires identity and constitution choices‚Äîfor example, person versus body, organism versus matter. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:212:| `KIND` | Type/class; sometimes UFO `Kind` | P | Major false-friend risk. NSM means ‚Äúthings of one kind‚Äù; UFO `Kind` is specifically a rigid sortal supplying identity. Most NSM `KIND` uses justify only `Type`, not `ufo:Kind`. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:213:| `PART` | General parthood | A/P | Strong anchor for a part-whole relation. It does not entail UFO‚Äôs reflexivity, transitivity, supplementation, category restrictions, essential parthood, membership, componenthood or functional dependence. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:214:| `THIS` | Demonstrative reference | √ò/P | Maps to discourse anchoring or a referring expression, not an ontological category. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:215:| `THE-SAME` | Identity or sameness relation | P | Can motivate an equality/coreference assertion, but natural-language qualitative sameness and numerical identity must be separated. The profile‚Äôs referent mechanism helps but does not supply cross-world identity. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:216:| `OTHER~ELSE~ANOTHER` | Distinctness/inequality | P | Can support `x ‚âÝ y` in a fixed context. It does not establish an identity criterion. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:217:| `ONE`, `TWO` | Cardinality | A | Good anchors for exact small cardinalities in an executable logic, subject to scope and unique-name assumptions. They are logical resources, not UFO categories. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:219:| `MUCH~MANY`, `LITTLE~FEW` | Vague quantity/cardinality regions | P | Require contextual thresholds or quality regions. UFO does not provide those thresholds automatically. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:220:| `GOOD`, `BAD` | Evaluative quality/value or judgment | P | May be modeled through qualities, value regions, goals or normative descriptions. They are context-sensitive evaluators, not generic UFO `Quality` instances by themselves. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:222:| `THINK` | Mental event/state or intentional moment | P | UFO-C can model a mental moment or its manifestation. The prime does not settle whether the referent is an enduring mode, a state or an event occurrence. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:223:| `KNOW` | Belief/knowledge state with factive content | P | UFO-C has belief and propositional content, but belief is not knowledge and does not automatically encode factivity or justification. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:224:| `WANT`, `DON'T-WANT` | Desire/aversion | A/P | Strong anchors for UFO-C desire-like intentional moments. Reification, inherence and propositional content require the UFO layer. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:227:| `SAY` | Communicative action/event | A/P | Usually aligns with an intentional action. UFO-C additionally models speaker intention, content, participants and possible social effects. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:229:| `TRUE` | Truth/satisfaction relation over propositional content | P/√ò | Better mapped to formal semantics than to a UFO base category. UFO-C propositions and situations can support satisfaction, but truth is not reducible to an entity class. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:230:| `DO` | Action or agent participation | A/P | Strong action anchor when the actor is intentional. NSM `DO` is broader than UFO-C intentional `Action`, so intentionality cannot be assumed from syntax alone. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:231:| `HAPPEN` | Event/perdurant | A | The strongest prime-to-UFO anchor. Even so, `HAPPEN(p)` is a predication; an ontology must separately reify or type the event and identify its participants and temporal parts. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:232:| `MOVE` | Movement/change event | A/P | A natural subtype of event/process. UFO classification still needs the mover, trajectory and event identity. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:234:| `THERE-IS` | Existence | A/P | Aligns with existential assertion. UFO‚Äôs modal and time-indexed existence predicates add machinery absent from the prime. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:235:| `BE-SPEC` | Classification, predication or attribution | P | Depending on the filler it may express type instantiation, identity, quality attribution or specification. It must not be compiled uniformly. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:236:| `IS-MINE` | Possession/ownership relation or social relator | P | Ownership commonly needs agents, norms, claims and a social relator. The prime supplies first-person possession, not UFO‚Äôs full ownership structure. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:237:| `LIVE` | Biological state/process | P | A domain type under event/enduring biological organization, not a UFO foundational category. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:245:| `NOT` | Logical negation | A | Maps to formal apparatus, not a UFO category. Open-world negation, explicit negative facts and failure-to-prove must remain distinct. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:246:| `MAYBE` | Epistemic/modal possibility | P | Does not choose UFO‚Äôs metaphysical possible-world modality, epistemic uncertainty or probabilistic confidence. |

item.text: The key distinction is emerging: RDF 1.2 can carry the higher-order-looking data, but named graphs alone do not give it modal semantics. The decisive answer will separate faithful reasoning over an explicitly represented finite Kripke frame (decidable) from unrestricted quantified-modal UFO semantics (not an OWL-RL+Horn capability).

item.text:
## Answer

**[well-established]** Every listed UFO-A construct can be represented as RDF 1.2 data, including statements about class membership and statements about those statements.  
**[well-established]** RDF 1.2, OWL 2 and ordinary N3 do not themselves assign possible-world semantics to named graphs or triple reifiers; RDF 1.2 explicitly leaves such dataset semantics to an extension. [RDF 1.2 Concepts](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf12-concepts/), [RDF 1.2 Semantics ¬ß10](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf12-semantics/#rdf-datasets), [N3](https://www.w3.org/TeamSubmission/n3/)

**[plausible]** All constructs are executable and decidable over a finite, explicitly enumerated Kripke frame if the engine adds world-indexed predicates, explicit negative membership or stratified negation, and integrity constraints.  
**[well-established]** Full reference-UFO semantics over arbitrary possible worlds and arbitrary identity criteria is not reducible to decidable OWL-RL plus ordinary Horn N3.

### Shared RDF 1.2 carrier

**[plausible]** A concrete encoding can use either named graphs or RDF 1.2 reifiers:

```trig
:w1 a ufo:PossibleWorld ; ufo:assertionGraph :w1g .
:w1 ufo:accessible :w2 .
:alice ufo:existsIn :w1, :w2 .

GRAPH :w1g {
  :alice rdf:type :Person .
}

:r1 rdf:reifies <<( :alice rdf:type :Person )>> ;
    ufo:trueIn :w1 .

:r2 rdf:reifies <<( :alice rdf:type :Person )>> ;
    ufo:falseIn :w2 .
```

**[well-established]** RDF 1.2 triple terms need not be asserted, and reifiers are deliberately suitable for describing situations in which propositions hold. [RDF 1.2 Concepts ¬ß1.5](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf12-concepts/#section-triple-terms)

**[plausible]** The engine should compile either representation into:

```text
exists(x,w)       holds(x,T,w)       notHolds(x,T,w)
accessible(w,v)   subtype(T,U)       identityProvider(T,K)
```

**[plausible]** A finite-world profile must declare its world set and membership data complete; otherwise absence of `holds` cannot soundly mean `notHolds`.

### Per-construct result

| Construct | RDF 1.2 encoding or extension needed | Decidable? | Notes |
|---|---|---:|---|
| **Kind** | **[plausible]** `:Person a owl:Class, ufo:Kind; ufo:rigidity ufo:Rigid; ufo:identityCriterion :PersonIC`. Add exact-one identity-criterion/provider SHACL constraints and the rigidity rule below. | **[well-established]** Yes for OWL structure and finite-world validation. **[well-established]** No general guarantee for arbitrary identity formulae. | **[well-established]** A Kind is rigid, sortal and identity-supplying. [OntoUML Kind](https://ontouml.readthedocs.io/en/latest/classes/sortals/kind/) |
| **Subkind** | **[plausible]** `:Employee rdfs:subClassOf :Person; a ufo:Subkind; ufo:identityProvider :Person; ufo:rigidity ufo:Rigid`. Rule/SHACL requires exactly one inherited provider. | **[well-established]** Yes under the finite profile. | **[well-established]** A Subkind is rigid and inherits rather than supplies identity. [OntoUML Subkind](https://ontouml.readthedocs.io/en/latest/classes/sortals/subkind/) |
| **Role** | **[plausible]** `:Student a ufo:Role; rdfs:subClassOf :Person; ufo:identityProvider :Person; ufo:condition :EnrollmentCondition`. Add anti-rigidity plus a world-indexed external-dependence rule such as `holds(x,Student,w) ‚Üí mediatedBy(x,r,w)`. | **[plausible]** Yes with finite worlds and explicit witnesses; implementable with SHACL-SPARQL or stratified Datalog/N3. | **[well-established]** A Role is an anti-rigid sortal, inherits one identity principle and is relationally conditioned. [OntoUML Role](https://ontouml.readthedocs.io/en/latest/classes/sortals/role/) |
| **Phase** | **[plausible]** `:Adult a ufo:Phase; rdfs:subClassOf :Person; ufo:intrinsicCondition :Age18Plus; ufo:memberOfPartition :LifePhase`. Encode the partition using `owl:disjointUnionOf` or SHACL completeness/disjointness constraints, plus anti-rigidity. | **[plausible]** Yes for finite worlds; OWL-RL may require compiled partition rules or SHACL validation. | **[well-established]** A Phase is an anti-rigid sortal determined intrinsically and normally belongs to a complete disjoint phase partition. [OntoUML Phase](https://ontouml.readthedocs.io/en/latest/classes/sortals/phase/) |
| **RoleMixin** | **[plausible]** `:Customer a ufo:RoleMixin, owl:Class; ufo:rigidity ufo:AntiRigid; ufo:sortality ufo:NonSortal; ufo:condition :PurchaseRelation`. Require qualifying subtypes from multiple identity providers and external dependence. | **[plausible]** Yes under finite validation. | **[well-established]** A RoleMixin is anti-rigid, non-sortal, relationally dependent and does not supply a common identity principle. [OntoUML RoleMixin](https://ontouml.readthedocs.io/en/latest/classes/nonsortals/rolemixin/) |
| **Category** | **[plausible]** `:PhysicalObject a ufo:Category; ufo:rigidity ufo:Rigid; ufo:sortality ufo:NonSortal`. SHACL checks that it supplies no identity criterion and may subsume types with different providers. | **[well-established]** Yes under finite validation. | **[well-established]** A Category is a rigid non-sortal aggregating instances governed by different identity principles. [OntoUML Category](https://ontouml.readthedocs.io/en/latest/classes/nonsortals/category/) |
| **Mixin** | **[plausible]** `:LuxuryGood a ufo:Mixin; ufo:rigidity ufo:SemiRigid; ufo:sortality ufo:NonSortal`. Apply the semi-rigidity witness rules below. | **[plausible]** Yes for finite complete data using stratified negation/SHACL; not by positive Horn rules alone. | **[well-established]** A Mixin is semi-rigid and non-sortal: essential for some instances and contingent for others. [OntoUML Mixin](https://ontouml.readthedocs.io/en/latest/classes/nonsortals/mixin/) |
| **Identity** | **[plausible]** Represent the provider and criterion as first-class resources: `T ufo:identityProvider K`; `K ufo:identityCriterion IC`; `IC ufo:condition { ‚Ä¶ }`. A safe N3/Datalog criterion may derive `sameContinuant(x,y)` or carefully controlled `owl:sameAs`. | **[well-established]** Decidable when each criterion is a finite, range-restricted Datalog rule. **[well-established]** Arbitrary quantified criteria, functions or unrestricted existential N3 lose that guarantee. | **[well-established]** OWL `HasKey` covers some extensional criteria but not arbitrary persistence/identity principles. **[plausible]** Cross-world identity should normally use `sameContinuant`, not collapse situation records with `owl:sameAs`. |
| **Rigidity** | **[plausible]** Add dataset-aware rule: `violationRigid(T,x,w) ‚Üê rigid(T), instanceSomewhere(x,T), exists(x,w), notHolds(x,T,w)`. With closed finite membership, `not holds` may replace explicit `notHolds`. | **[well-established]** Yes over a finite explicit frame. **[well-established]** Positive OWL-RL/Horn alone cannot derive a violation from absence. | **[well-established]** This implements ‚Äúevery instance is in T in every represented situation in which it exists‚Äù; it does not quantify over unrepresented possible worlds. [OntoUML Rigidity](https://ontouml.readthedocs.io/en/latest/theory/rigidity/) |
| **Anti-rigidity** | **[plausible]** For every `x` ever in `T`, require a reachable witness `w` with `exists(x,w)` and `notHolds(x,T,w)`. Detect missing witnesses using SHACL-SPARQL `FILTER NOT EXISTS` or stratified negation. | **[plausible]** Yes on finite data. **[well-established]** Not expressible as ordinary monotone Horn materialization without supplied witnesses, negation or existential model generation. | **[well-established]** One counterexample individual is insufficient: anti-rigidity requires possible cessation for every instance. |
| **Semi-rigidity** | **[plausible]** Derive `essentialFor(T,x)` when no accessible counterworld exists and `contingentFor(T,y)` when one does; require at least one witness of each kind. | **[plausible]** Yes with finite complete worlds and stratified negation. **[well-established]** Not derivable in positive OWL-RL alone. | **[well-established]** Semi-rigidity is not merely an annotation meaning ‚Äúneither proved rigid nor proved anti-rigid‚Äù; it requires both essential and contingent instances. |

### Exact extension needed

**[plausible]** The minimal extension to an OWL-RL+N3 engine is a finite-modal Datalog layer with:

- **[plausible]** quotation/reifier or named-graph compilation into `holds/notHolds/exists/accessibility`;
- **[plausible]** integrity-constraint heads such as `violation(...)`;
- **[plausible]** explicit negation, or stratified negation over a declared-complete finite world set;
- **[plausible]** witness checking for anti- and semi-rigidity;
- **[plausible]** range-restricted identity-criterion rules.

**[well-established]** OWL 2 RL reasoning is polynomial-time, while unrestricted N3 with existential rule heads does not inherit that bound; decidability requires a restricted Datalog or known decidable existential-rule fragment. [OWL 2 Profiles](https://www.w3.org/TR/owl2-profiles/), [Existential N3](https://www.cambridge.org/core/product/identifier/S1471068425000055/type/journal_article)

**[plausible]** SHACL-SPARQL is the simplest implementation for validation; finite stratified Datalog/N3 is preferable if proof-producing inference is required. [SHACL 1.2 SPARQL](https://www.w3.org/TR/shacl12-sparql/)

## Family verdict

**Partly.**

- **[well-established]** **Yes** as RDF 1.2 representation: all constructs, formulas, worlds and statements-about-statements can be carried without a reification barrier.
- **[plausible]** **Yes** as decidable executable validation over a finite, explicitly complete set of situations, using the extension above.
- **[well-established]** **No** as a semantics-preserving implementation of unrestricted reference-UFO modal quantification inside ordinary OWL-RL+N3.
- **[plausible]** Full arbitrary-world semantics requires a genuine quantified-modal or higher-order prover; that extension is implementable as a separate regime, but not generally decidable and not an OWL-RL materializer.

**[well-established]** Therefore the prior ‚ÄúRDF cannot express statements about statements‚Äù rationale is incorrect. **[well-established]** The remaining boundary is modal entailment and completeness over possible worlds‚Äînot RDF reification.
